1: Command to set up memorial stones: YHWH's command to take twelve stones from the Jordan riverbed (Joshua 4:2-3) establishes a physical memorial of the crossing. Memorial stones (avanim, H68; matzevot when standing) were the standard ANE method of commemorating significant events: treaty conclusions, military victories, and divine encounters were marked by stone monuments. The standing stones of Gezer, the stele of Mesha, and the boundary stones of Mesopotamia all served this function. Israel's use of stones for memorial is consistent with broader ANE practice but theologically redirected: the stones do not contain divine power or bear inscriptions of royal achievement; they serve as pedagogical triggers for future generations' questions.
2: Twelve men carrying twelve stones: The one-man-per-tribe selection for the stone-carrying (Joshua 4:2, cf. Joshua 3:12) ensures that the memorial is a tribal, not merely individual, act. Each tribe's representative carried one stone on his shoulder (Joshua 4:5 — 'take up each man a stone on his shoulder') — a physical act of communal labor. The memorial would not be the work of priests or leaders alone but of the whole tribal confederation. The stones came from where the priests' feet stood — the holiest ground in the crossing, the spot where YHWH's presence was most concentrated. The twelve stones taken from that ground transferred the holiness of the encounter into the permanent monument.
3: Lodging place at Gilgal: The lodging place (Hebrew malon) where the stones were to be erected was Gilgal — the base camp of the conquest. Gilgal (near modern Khirbet el-Mafjer, 2 km east of Jericho) became Israel's headquarters during the early conquest phase: circumcision was performed there (Joshua 5:2-9), the Passover was kept there (Joshua 5:10), the Gibeonite deception was discovered there (Joshua 9:6), and it served as the base for multiple campaigns. The word Gilgal is etymologically connected to galal (to roll) — the rolling away of Egypt's reproach (Joshua 5:9) became the etymology. Gilgal was a sacred site before Israel arrived; it is mentioned as a boundary marker in several passages (1 Samuel 7:16; Joshua 10:8).
4: Joshua calling the twelve men: The logistical detail of Joshua calling each appointed man (Joshua 4:4) grounds the narrative in the specifics of military command. A general calling individually appointed men for a specific task reflects organized command structure. The appointment of twelve specific men for the stone-carrying was not improvised but had been planned (Joshua 3:12) — Joshua knew who would carry the stones before the crossing. Pre-planning the memorial monument before the miracle occurred reflects the theological confidence of preparation: YHWH had promised the crossing would succeed, so the monument's bearers were already identified.
5: "Pass on before the Ark of YHWH": The command 'pass on before the Ark of YHWH your God into the middle of the Jordan' (Joshua 4:5) sent the twelve men back into the riverbed while the priests still stood there — back into the miracle itself to retrieve the memorial stones. They were to pass 'before the Ark' — approaching the divine presence's location. This act required a form of the courage the priests had shown: entering a (currently dry but miraculously held) riverbed with the Ark still standing on its floor. The stones they carried back were literally taken from beneath YHWH's throne-presence — making them the most sacred commemorative objects imaginable.
6: "That this may be a sign among you": The pedagogical purpose of the memorial stones (Joshua 4:6-7) employs the standard catechetical formula of Deuteronomy: the monument exists to trigger children's questions ('What do these stones mean to you?') which give parents the opportunity to transmit the tradition orally. Deuteronomy 6:20-25 uses the same pattern for the Torah commands. The monument is not self-interpreting — it requires transmitted speech to give it meaning. This reflects an oral culture's approach to memory: objects anchor stories, and stories are transmitted through interrogation and response. The physical memorial and the verbal tradition work together; neither alone suffices.
7: "Memorial forever": 'These stones shall be to the people of Israel a memorial forever' (Joshua 4:7) — the zikaron le'olam (memorial for ever) is a liturgical claim that transcends any particular generation. Like the Passover memorial (Exodus 12:14 — 'a memorial throughout your generations'), the Jordan stones were intended not for a temporary commemoration but for a permanent sign. The irony is that the exact location of Gilgal and the memorial stones has been disputed and possibly lost — the le'olam was conditional on continued remembrance. The text's aspiration that these stones would speak to every future generation was realized not through the physical stones (which are gone) but through this very narrative, the textual memorial that has outlasted the physical one.
8: Israelites did as Joshua commanded: The compliance formula 'the people of Israel did as Joshua commanded' (Joshua 4:8) is a standard narrative marker in Joshua-Judges for obedience that produces blessing, contrasted with non-compliance that produces disaster. The book of Joshua's first half is structured around this pattern: obedience (Joshua 3-6, including stone memorial) → blessing; disobedience (Joshua 7, Achan) → defeat; renewed obedience (Joshua 8) → victory. The memorial stones' erection is part of the obedience cycle that structures the conquest narrative.
9: Twelve stones in the Jordan itself: In addition to the twelve stones erected at Gilgal (Joshua 4:3,8), Joshua set up twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan itself, 'where the feet of the priests who bore the Ark of the Covenant stood' (Joshua 4:9) — a second, underwater monument. This submerged monument would be visible only at low water seasons, a reminder of the miracle visible from the river itself. The dual monument (riverbed + Gilgal) serves different audiences: travelers crossing the Jordan at low water would see the submerged stones; the main memorial at Gilgal served the community. The text notes 'they are there to this day' — a phrase that anchors the account to a known contemporary reality.
10: Timing: priests in the Jordan until everything was finished: 'The priests who bore the Ark stood in the midst of the Jordan until everything was finished' (Joshua 4:10) — the duration of the crossing for an entire people was significant. Modern estimates suggest the full Israelite population (if numbering hundreds of thousands) could have taken many hours to cross, even through a wide, dry riverbed. The priests stood in the Jordan throughout, maintaining the miraculous dam upstream by their presence. 'The people hurried and crossed' (Joshua 4:10) adds urgency: they were aware of the precarious situation — standing waters held back only by YHWH's command could return at any moment.
11: Ark crossing last: the rear guard: 'The Ark of YHWH and the priests crossed over before the people' (Joshua 4:11) — the Ark crossed last, after all the people had passed. In military procession theology, the divine standard marching last served as a rear guard, ensuring no stragglers were lost. The same theology appears in the Exodus: the pillar of cloud moved behind Israel to stand between them and the pursuing Egyptians (Exodus 14:19-20). YHWH's presence both led (front of the Ark procession in the Jordan) and protected (behind, as rear guard when the Ark finally crossed). The Ark's final crossing precipitated the return of the waters (Joshua 4:18) — a dramatic confirmation that the miracle's cause was YHWH's presence with the Ark, not a natural phenomenon.
12: Transjordanian tribes crossing armed: 'The sons of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh crossed over armed before the people of Israel' (Joshua 4:12) — in battle formation as Moses had commanded (Numbers 32:32; Joshua 1:14). The Transjordanian warriors marched as vanguard, the most exposed position in any military crossing. Their presence at the front fulfilled their obligation (Numbers 32:17: 'we will go armed before the people of Israel') and demonstrated that Transjordanian tribes were full partners in the conquest. The approximately 40,000 armed warriors crossing the Jordan represented a substantial fighting force — the core of the conquest army.
13: 40,000 armed for war: The 40,000 armed men (Joshua 4:13) is a smaller number than the census figures in Numbers (which give Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh over 100,000 adult males). The 40,000 may represent the select fighting men left behind to cross (rather than the total population, which stayed east of the Jordan) — the mobile combat force rather than the full tribal roll. Alternative interpretations read the large numbers in Numbers as military units ('thousands' = elef = a military unit of variable size) rather than literal counts. Whatever the specific count, the 40,000 represent a genuine and significant military force, consistent with the larger narrative of conquest.
14: Joshua exalted in all Israel's sight: 'YHWH exalted Joshua in the sight of all Israel' (Joshua 4:14) — the fulfillment of Joshua 3:7's promise. The Jordan miracle established Joshua's prophetic authority in the same way that Moses's plagues established Moses's authority before Israel. In the ancient world, the leader whose God performed visible wonders was vindicated as YHWH's genuine representative. The people's response — 'they feared him as they had feared Moses all the days of his life' (Joshua 4:14) — grants Joshua the same reverent authority that Moses commanded. This legitimation was necessary for the conquest to proceed: Israel needed to follow Joshua into an unprecedented and terrifying military campaign.
15: YHWH's command to Joshua: The narrative of YHWH's ongoing speech to Joshua throughout the crossing narrative (Joshua 3:7-8; Joshua 4:1-3; Joshua 4:15-16) reflects the prophetic model of leadership: Joshua acts only on received divine word. This contrasts with ancient Near Eastern military commanders who acted on their own initiative and credited their victories to divine favor after the fact. In Israel's theological framework, the leader receives the divine word first and then acts — the sequence matters. The theological claim is not merely that YHWH approved Joshua's decisions but that Joshua's decisions were YHWH's decisions communicated through prophetic channel.
16: Command to the priests to come up from the Jordan: The command to the priests to 'come up from the Jordan' (Joshua 4:16) is issued through Joshua, who received it from YHWH (Joshua 4:15-16) — the chain of command runs YHWH → Joshua → priests. This command chain reflects the hierarchical structure of Israel's covenant community: the divine word comes to the leader, who transmits it to the functionaries. The priests did not receive independent divine communication for leaving the riverbed — they waited for Joshua's command, which waited on YHWH's command. This chain of authority would be violated by Achan's independent action in Joshua 7 and by the Transjordanian tribes' altar-building in Joshua 22, both of which triggered crises.
17: Joshua commanded the priests: 'Joshua commanded the priests' (Joshua 4:17) — the verb tzavah (to command) is used consistently for authoritative directive speech from leader to subordinate. Joshua commanded the priests as the supreme military-religious authority of the campaign; the priests obeyed as subordinates executing received orders. The authority structure of the conquest is clear: Joshua is not merely a military general but the covenant leader whose authority encompasses the religious functionaries (Levitical priests) as well as the military and civil dimensions of Israel's life.
18: Waters returned as soon as priests' feet lifted: 'When the soles of the priests' feet were lifted up on dry ground, the waters of the Jordan returned to their place and overflowed all its banks as before' (Joshua 4:18) — the miracle reversed at the same moment it had begun: when feet touched the water (Joshua 3:15), it stopped; when feet left the water, it returned. The symmetry of the miracle (entry and exit of priests' feet as the trigger) confirms the theological logic: the miracle was YHWH's response to his servants' obedient steps, not a mechanical natural event. The immediate return of the Jordan flood waters to 'all its banks as before' confirmed the miracle was not a gradual natural dam-release but a sharp, sudden reversal.
19: The tenth of the first month at Gilgal: 'The people came up from the Jordan on the tenth day of the first month' (Joshua 4:19) — the tenth of Nisan was precisely the day on which the Passover lamb was to be selected (Exodus 12:3: 'on the tenth day of this month they shall take every man a lamb'). The calendar alignment is precise and likely intentional: Israel entered the land on the day of Passover lamb-selection, four days before the Passover feast (Joshua 5:10). The arrival at Gilgal on the tenth of Nisan placed Israel in position to celebrate the first Passover in the land on the fourteenth (Joshua 5:10) — the conquest of Canaan begins in the liturgical framework of Exodus commemoration.
20: The twelve stones set up at Gilgal: The erection of the twelve Jordan stones at Gilgal (Joshua 4:20) was the physical act that transformed the encampment into a sanctuary of memory. Gilgal ('circle of stones') may itself be named for this stone circle, or the name may have pre-existed and given the event its meaning. Circular stone monuments were common in ancient Canaan and the surrounding world — standing stone circles at Gezer, the megaliths of the Golan Heights, and Stonehenge-type structures across the ancient world all reflect the human instinct to mark sacred spaces with enduring stone. Israel's Gilgal stone circle was not a pagan sacred site but a repurposed geography: the circle of stones that had previously served Canaanite purposes became Israel's memorial of YHWH's conquest-crossing.
21: The pedagogical question: "What do these stones mean?": The anticipation of children's questions (Joshua 4:21-22) is the Deuteronomic catechetical pattern (Deuteronomy 6:20; Exodus 12:26-27; Joshua 13:14). The memorial object (stones) triggers the child's inquiry; the parent responds with the narrative. In oral cultures, memory is transmitted through story, and stories are elicited by visible prompts. The stones did not contain inscriptions explaining their meaning (unlike Assyrian royal stele); their meaning was maintained through verbal transmission across generations. The entire educational theory presupposes an active tradition of telling: unless parents told the Jordan story repeatedly when their children pointed to the stones, the memorial would become meaningless.
22: "Israel crossed the Jordan on dry ground": The response formula 'you shall let your children know, Israel crossed over this Jordan on dry ground' (Joshua 4:22) employs the exodus parallel: 'dry ground' (yabbashah) is the technical term used in the sea-crossing (Exodus 14:22,29). The repetition of the same phrase for both events was deliberate: parents teaching their children about Gilgal were teaching them to understand the Jordan crossing as a second Exodus, and the land of Canaan as a new wilderness entry. The conquest generation was the new generation that completed the exodus journey that Egypt's generation had failed to finish.
23: "As YHWH your God did to the Red Sea": The explicit comparison to the Red Sea crossing (Joshua 4:23) — 'YHWH your God dried up the waters of the Jordan before you until you had crossed over, as YHWH your God did to the Red Sea' — is the interpretive key the parents were to provide. The Jordan miracle was not merely a useful engineering event but a deliberate echo of the founding miracle of Israel's existence. The two 'dry ground' crossings bracket the wilderness period: the sea-crossing began the exodus; the Jordan-crossing completed it. The catechism teaches children to see their history as a coherent pattern of divine deliverance, not a series of disconnected events.
24: "That all the peoples of the earth may know": 'That all the peoples of the earth may know the hand of YHWH is mighty' (Joshua 4:24) extends the purpose of the Jordan miracle beyond Israel to a universal witness. The 'hand of YHWH' (yad YHWH) is the standard expression for YHWH's powerful, visible intervention in history — the Exodus plagues, the Red Sea crossing, and now the Jordan crossing were all manifestations of the divine 'hand.' The claim that Canaanite knowledge of YHWH's power would cause them to 'fear YHWH forever' (Joshua 4:24) is the missionary dimension of the conquest: YHWH's reputation established through Israel's history is the basis for all nations' encounter with him. Rahab's confession (Joshua 2:11) is already this fear-of-YHWH-based-on-known-history realized in one Canaanite life.
